text: Welcome to This Is My Architecture. Today I'm joined by Tom from Veritas. Welcome Tom.
Thanks Elvis, appreciate the opportunity. Tell us what does Veritas do? So Veritas is an enterprise
data management company that focuses on three things, availability of applications and the
storage they're consuming, which is what we're going to talk about today. The other two pieces
are protection, protecting that data and making sure that in the event that
something catastrophic happens you can recover that data. And then third piece
insight into that data, you know lots of data out there and customers want to be
able to more efficiently manage that data. All right, well let's talk about the
availability piece. Tell me tell us about that. So probably the best way to
describe InfoScale is a kind of a bridge between applications and
infrastructure. So we're essentially abstracting the complexity of the
infrastructure from clustering perspective, from a storage perspective, and
making it directly accessible to that application. So if we have let's say for
instance a business critical service as a large beverage company, one of the largest
beverage companies in the world, their business critical service was SAP on the
back end. They use SAP for production, delivery, sales, distribution, everything.
What they wanted to do, and this is a CIO initiative as many of us have seen,
we're going to move in a cloud for cost-saving purposes, right? So we went in and
worked with a system integrator initially and they were told that it was going to
take three years to make this transition into AWS. We came in and says, no, there's a number of
things that we can do to drastically reduce that time and ended up doing it in eight
months. How we did that was focusing on three key areas. First, the migration. How do we
get there? And when you're going to migrate, most of the time you're looking at moving
that data set from on-prem into cloud. So InfoScale has a global file system that
enables us to actually replicate data from on-prem into cloud. One of the things that we
noticed immediately is since we are at the file system level and have application
intelligence is that they had 100 terabytes of capacity. The initial system integrator
was thinking, well, I got a copy 100 terabytes over there. He says, no. If you actually look
at the data sets in 100 terabytes, there's only 30 terabytes of data. So that was a drastic savings.
So you helped them, or InfoScale helped them move that easily from on-premises into a version
of InfoScale on AWS. Correct. Okay. Along with that storage migration piece, the second area that
they ran into a challenge with was costs. They wanted to put the entire SAP and Oracle on the back end
into performance storage, which is somewhat expensive. So since we do have this global file
system, we were able to show them how that we could carve things up. So if you've got, you know,
10% of your data that you want in highly performant storage, you can do that. You got 60% and just sit
in standard HDD. And then you've got 30% that you're going to put in S3. Got it.
In a single file system, we can carve that up with policies that dictate how much data stays
in one persistent state at another time, and then eventually tear that off.
Yeah, that makes sense. So you're using the various storage tiers that AWS offers through
the intelligence and InfoScale to tear that in a cost-effective way. And to help customers
save money. In a performance way.
When they looked at it initially, it broke their TCO model. But when we said, no, Amazon offers
all kinds of storage, and we can help you carve that up based on the right fit for function
for that data set. Right. So then you got that into one region. Can you scale across regions
with InfoScale? So that comes back to the availability piece. The second concern, they wanted the same
level of availability that they had on-prem, and they were replicating data all over the globe. So they were
interested initially in zone-to-zone replication, which we can do synchronously through this clustered
file system. And then also for disaster recovery purposes, they wanted to be able to replicate
this to another region. So since our intelligence within InfoScale really sits at this business
critical service or application level, we understand the relationship between the application and the
data sets that they're being consumed. On that clustered file system that supports any type of storage on the
back end, we're able to understand the relationship of that storage to the application. So in the event
that there is any inconsistencies in terms of replication, you have a failure, you immediately want to cut things
off from writing to those data sets to avoid corruption. That's what InfoScale delivers.
Got it. And then what if you still needed on-premises for, say, compliance reasons or some other reason like that?
So many customers are concerned for regulatory purposes or potentially latency issues, and they can't put
everything into the cloud. Or if you're a financial institution in the United Kingdom, for instance,
you have to be able to failover from on-prem into cloud and back and be able to show that. So we can
actually do that through an automated process. We actually call it fire drill, and it's essentially a
fire drill. You don't have to do anything with your production application. You can failover into the cloud,
failover out of the cloud, show the auditor the compliance report, and we can also do this on outpost.
If you don't want to move that data out, you can have a hybrid cloud configuration with some of your data
data on-prem and bursting capabilities into the cloud. Neat. Well, thanks for sharing your architecture with us today, Tom.
And thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
